context.description: A philosophical discussion of psychoactive substances such as LSD and DMT argues that they can produce shared, substance-specific effects and apparently impossible geometries. It contrasts these reported experiences with the limits of ordinary three-dimensional imagination and begins an example about everyday perception of objects and their hidden sides.
normalization_notes: No chapter heading or book title is visible on the extracted page.

rows.paragraph_text: Psychoactive substances can produce effects that seem shared and substance-specific, not merely subjective.
rows.text_content: Substances such as LSD and especially DMT appear to produce extraordinary effects that seem hard to explain from the outside.

rows.paragraph_text: LSD and especially DMT are used to illustrate the limits of three-dimensional imagination, including impossible-seeming geometries.
rows.text_content: A tesseract is used as an example of how human thought is limited to three-dimensional representations and cannot truly visualize four spatial dimensions.

rows.paragraph_text: Reported DMT experiences include geometric forms that users later cannot fully reconstruct but still regard as genuinely understood during the experience.
rows.text_content: The text defends reports of impossible geometric patterns during DMT and other hallucinogen experiences as real phenomena that should not be dismissed outright.

rows.paragraph_text: An everyday perception example begins with seeing only one side of an object while knowing it also has a reverse side.
rows.text_content: The discussion turns to an ordinary case of perception without substances, introducing the idea that objects are normally experienced from only one visible side.
